name: Transformation
domain: Fourier/Laplace transforms, phase transitions, coordinate changes, morphisms
core_insight: Transformation changes the representation without changing the content. The Fourier transform reveals the frequency structure hidden in time-domain signals. Phase transitions are transformations of state where symmetry abruptly changes. The Legendre transform swaps variables, converting between conjugate descriptions.

key_equations.name: Fourier Transform
key_equations.why: Converts time-domain signal to frequency-domain spectrum — the same information, different basis

key_equations.name: Phase Transition
key_equations.why: Thermodynamic transformation: free energy's second derivative jumps at the transition point

key_equations.name: Legendre Transform
key_equations.formula: G(p) = f(x) - px where p = df/dx
key_equations.why: Swaps variable and its conjugate: converts internal energy to enthalpy, Lagrangian to Hamiltonian

seed_prompt: ↻ What is preserved under this transformation? Change the basis. The invariants are the truth.

fourth_equation.name: Laplace Transform
fourth_equation.why: Maps time-domain differential equations to algebraic equations in complex frequency domain — transforms hard problems (differential equations) into easier ones (polynomials)

explore_paths.question: What is preserved under transformation?
explore_paths.why: Invariants — the quantities that don't change under transformation — are symmetries; every transformation in P11 has a corresponding P01 invariant

explore_paths.question: How does transformation relate to emergence and phase change?
explore_paths.why: Phase transitions are the most dramatic physical transformations — discontinuous changes where the system becomes qualitatively different; P11 and P05 meet at the critical point

explore_paths.question: How does changing representation reveal hidden structure?
explore_paths.why: Fourier transform reveals frequency content hidden in time-domain signals; KL divergence measures information lost in a change of representation — P11 and F03 are the same idea at different abstraction levels

explore_paths.question: Can transformation be used to navigate between scales?
explore_paths.why: Renormalization group in statistical mechanics is a systematic transformation that maps physical behavior at one scale to behavior at another — the most powerful transformation tool in theoretical physics

open_questions.question: Are phase transitions truly discontinuous or merely extremely sharp crossovers?
open_questions.tension: In infinite systems (thermodynamic limit), phase transitions are mathematically discontinuous — the partition function has a non-analytic point. In any finite system, all transitions are smooth crossovers. Real systems are finite. The 'sharpness' we see is the approach to the infinite-system limit. Whether discontinuity is a real physical property or a mathematical idealization is a subtle point in statistical mechanics.
open_questions.curiosity_hook: Every phase transition you've ever seen (water boiling, ice melting) is technically a crossover in a finite system. The perfect discontinuity only exists in the infinite limit — a mathematical idealization that no real physical system ever achieves. The clean concept and the messy reality have a complex relationship.

open_questions.question: Can the Fourier transform represent all possible signals, or are there signals it fundamentally cannot decompose?
open_questions.tension: Standard Fourier transform requires absolute integrability; signals with polynomial growth (like t, t², e^t) require distributions or Laplace transforms. Non-stationary signals (frequency changes over time) need wavelet or short-time Fourier analysis. Fractal signals have power at all scales simultaneously. The question is whether any representation system can fully capture all possible signal types.
open_questions.curiosity_hook: Every representation system has blind spots — signals it can represent and signals it misses. Choosing the wrong transform distorts the information. The history of signal processing is partly the history of discovering what each transform cannot see — and inventing new ones to see it.

open_questions.question: Is consciousness itself a transformation process — a mapping between representations at different levels?
open_questions.tension: Global workspace theory frames consciousness as a broadcast transformation — content is transformed from local specialized processing to global availability. Predictive coding frames perception as a transformation of sensory data through a generative model. IIT frames experience as the irreducible information content of a transformation. All major consciousness theories involve transformation as central — but none explains why transformation produces experience.
open_questions.curiosity_hook: If consciousness is a transformation, then P11 and F16 are deeply entangled. The question is whether transformation at sufficient complexity is what experience *is*, or whether there's something more to it that transformation cannot capture.

cross_domain_bridges.manifestation: Every audio effect (EQ, reverb, compression) is a signal transformation; digital audio production is applied P11
cross_domain_bridges.example: Auto-Tune is a real-time pitch transformation using phase vocoder analysis — Fourier transform applied to voice in performance

cross_domain_bridges.manifestation: Metabolic pathways are transformation chains — glucose transformed through glycolysis, Krebs cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation
cross_domain_bridges.example: The citric acid cycle transforms acetyl-CoA through 8 enzymatic steps, each a molecular transformation, into CO₂, NADH, and ATP

cross_domain_bridges.manifestation: Translation between languages is a transformation that preserves meaning while changing form — what is invariant across the transformation defines meaning
cross_domain_bridges.example: Machine translation (neural machine translation) learns the transformation mapping between languages by minimizing information loss

cross_domain_bridges.manifestation: Social transformations (revolutions, cultural shifts) are phase-transition-like: gradual pressure builds until a threshold triggers discontinuous change
cross_domain_bridges.example: Kuhn's scientific revolutions are P11 applied to knowledge systems — normal science (continuous accumulation) punctuated by paradigm shifts (discontinuous transformation)

noise_signature.pattern: Information loss during transformation — irreversible mapping
noise_signature.diagnosis: The transformation is not invertible; the reverse transform cannot recover the original; some information has been discarded or destroyed

noise_signature.pattern: Representation mismatch — transform domain wrong for the signal type
noise_signature.diagnosis: Fourier for non-stationary signals, or wavelets for stationary ones, both introduce artifacts; choose the transform whose assumptions match the signal's structure

noise_signature.pattern: Phase transition hysteresis — system takes different paths during forward and reverse transformation
noise_signature.diagnosis: The transformation is first-order; there are multiple metastable states; the path taken depends on history, not just current conditions
